edges.description: Failed lysosomal catabolism leads to storage of partially degraded heparan sulfate.
The same upstream enzymatic lesion is detected diagnostically as decreased activity of the subtype-specific lysosomal enzyme.
Heparan sulfate storage triggers a neuroinflammatory response in the brain.
Heparan sulfate oligosaccharides perturb synaptic vesicle protein homeostasis in cortical neurons.
Lysosomal heparan sulfate storage also has extraneural systemic effects, including musculoskeletal, hearing, respiratory, and cardiac involvement.
intermediate_mechanisms: extraneural glycosaminoglycan storage in somatic tissues

edges.description: Increased heparan sulfate reports the stored substrate in Sanfilippo syndrome.
Quantitative urinary glycosaminoglycan analysis is used as a diagnostic readout of Sanfilippo glycosaminoglycan storage.
GM2 ganglioside storage is a secondary biochemical abnormality in neurodegenerative MPS III brain pathology.
intermediate_mechanisms: secondary lysosomal lipid storage in affected brain regions

edges.description: The inflammatory cascade is part of the progressive brain pathology that culminates in severe neurologic decline.
Early synaptic dysfunction is a plausible mediator of the prominent early behavioral phenotype.
Progressive CNS degeneration produces severe intellectual disability.
Progressive CNS degeneration leads to loss of previously acquired developmental skills.
Sleep disturbance is a common neurologic-behavioral consequence of the progressive CNS disease process.
Autism-spectrum manifestations are part of the neurologic and behavioral output of progressive Sanfilippo CNS disease.
Behavioral problems are part of the neurobehavioral expression of the progressive CNS disease process.
Seizures are neurologic complications managed as part of the Sanfilippo clinical spectrum.
Feeding difficulties are managed among the later neurologic and systemic complications of Sanfilippo syndrome.
intermediate_mechanisms: progressive neurologic decline and multisystem involvement

edges.description: Glycosaminoglycan storage in somatic tissues contributes to coarse facial features.
Systemic glycosaminoglycan storage can affect skeletal development and produce dysostosis multiplex.
Joint stiffness is one of the musculoskeletal manifestations in the systemic branch.
Contractures are part of the milder musculoskeletal involvement in MPS III.
Scoliosis is part of the musculoskeletal systemic branch.
Hip dysplasia is one of the musculoskeletal manifestations in the systemic branch.
Hearing loss is part of the systemic Sanfilippo syndrome spectrum.
Respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections are part of systemic MPS III involvement and contribute to mortality.
Valvular thickening is a cardiac manifestation in the systemic branch.
Cardiac conduction defects are part of the systemic branch.
Decreased SGSH, NAGLU, HGSNAT, or GNS enzyme activity reports the root heparan-sulfate catabolic defect.
Elevated heparan sulfate reports the proximal lysosomal storage lesion.
Abnormal urinary glycosaminoglycan analysis reports the lysosomal glycosaminoglycan storage axis.
GM2 ganglioside storage reports secondary lysosomal storage in the MPS III CNS disease branch.

nodes.description: Cardiac valve thickening is part of Sanfilippo cardiac involvement.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Behavioral problems are a core part of the Sanfilippo neurobehavioral presentation.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Autism-spectrum manifestations are included among the neurologic and behavioral features of Sanfilippo syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Defects in the cardiac conduction system are part of Sanfilippo cardiac involvement.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Coarse facial features are part of the systemic mucopolysaccharidosis type III phenotype, although somatic features are usually milder than in MPS I and II.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000280 | Coarse facial features | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Sanfilippo syndrome includes early cortical synaptic dysfunction with abnormal synaptic vesicle and postsynaptic protein handling before overt neuronal loss becomes dominant.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathology in mouse models of mucopolysaccharidosis type I, IIIA and IIIB.
evidence.snippet: Quantitative immunohistochemistry showed significantly increased lysosomal compartment, GM2 ganglioside storage, neuroinflammation, decreased and mislocalised synaptic vesicle associated membrane protein, (VAMP2), and decreased post-synaptic protein, Homer-1, in layers II/III-VI of the primary motor, somatosensory and parietal cortex.

nodes.description: Loss of previously acquired developmental skills is a hallmark of progressive Sanfilippo syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Dysostosis multiplex can occur in Sanfilippo syndrome as part of the milder systemic skeletal branch of this heparan-sulfate storage disorder.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000943 | Dysostosis multiplex | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Feeding difficulties are part of the managed clinical complications in MPS III.

evidence.snippet: Treatment of manifestations: Supportive therapies for neurodevelopmental delays, hearing loss, and visual impairment; medications (rather than behavioral therapy) for psychiatric/behavioral issues; physical therapy and/or orthopedic management of musculoskeletal manifestations; and management as prescribed by consulting specialists for seizures, cardiac involvement, sleep disorders, feeding difficulties.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathology in mouse models of mucopolysaccharidosis type I, IIIA and IIIB.
evidence.snippet: Quantitative immunohistochemistry showed significantly increased lysosomal compartment, GM2 ganglioside storage, neuroinflammation, decreased and mislocalised synaptic vesicle associated membrane protein, (VAMP2), and decreased post-synaptic protein, Homer-1, in layers II/III-VI of the primary motor, somatosensory and parietal cortex.

readouts.interpretation: GM2 ganglioside storage reports secondary lysosomal storage in the MPS III CNS disease branch.

nodes.description: Hearing loss is a systemic manifestation in the Sanfilippo syndrome spectrum.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathology in mouse models of mucopolysaccharidosis type I, IIIA and IIIB.
evidence.snippet: Total heparan sulphate (HS), was significantly elevated, and abnormally N-, 6-O and 2-O sulphated compared to WT, potentially altering HS-dependent cellular functions.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated heparan sulfate reports the proximal lysosomal storage lesion.

nodes.description: Hip dysplasia is part of the milder systemic musculoskeletal involvement in MPS III.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Severe hyperactivity is a prominent early behavioral manifestation of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by progressive cognitive decline and severe hyperactivity, with relatively mild somatic features.

nodes.description: Progressive intellectual disability is one of the dominant central nervous system manifestations across the Sanfilippo spectrum.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Subtype-specific gene restoration strategies are in development to restore the missing lysosomal enzyme step and lower heparan sulfate storage. The strongest abstract-level mechanistic support in this curation comes from SGSH-directed type A models.

evidence.snippet: Although there is no global consensus for treatment, enzyme replacement therapy and gene therapy can provide appropriate results.

nodes.description: Contractures are part of the musculoskeletal branch of MPS III.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Joint stiffness is a systemic musculoskeletal manifestation in the Sanfilippo syndrome spectrum.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Partially degraded heparan sulfate accumulates within lysosomes and becomes the biochemical lesion that drives downstream brain pathology.

evidence.reference_title: Intracerebroventricular dosing of N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase in mucopolysaccharidosis IIIA mice reduces markers of brain lysosomal dysfunction.
evidence.snippet: Enzyme deficiency results in accumulation of partially degraded HS within lysosomes throughout the body, leading to a progressive severe neurological disease.

nodes.description: The root disease mechanism is deficiency of one of four lysosomal enzymes required for the stepwise degradation of heparan sulfate.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III, Sanfilippo syndrome) is a lysosomal storage disorder, caused by a deficiency in one of the four enzymes involved in the catabolism of glycosaminoglycan heparan sulfate.

The diagnosis of MPS III is established in a proband with suggestive clinical and laboratory findings in whom either biallelic pathogenic variants in one of four genes (GNS, HGSNAT, NAGLU, and SGSH) or deficiency of the respective lysosomal enzyme has been identified.

nodes.description: Brain heparan sulfate storage is accompanied by microglial, astrocytic, and cytokine evidence of neuroinflammation.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathology in mouse models of mucopolysaccharidosis type I, IIIA and IIIB.
evidence.snippet: Quantitative immunohistochemistry showed significantly increased lysosomal compartment, GM2 ganglioside storage, neuroinflammation, decreased and mislocalised synaptic vesicle associated membrane protein, (VAMP2), and decreased post-synaptic protein, Homer-1, in layers II/III-VI of the primary motor, somatosensory and parietal cortex.

nodes.description: The shared clinical trajectory across Sanfilippo syndrome is progressive central nervous system degeneration with worsening cognition, regression, and later premature death.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections are part of the systemic MPS III spectrum and can contribute to shortened survival.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Scoliosis can occur as part of the systemic musculoskeletal involvement.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).

nodes.description: Seizures are recognized neurologic complications requiring specialist management in MPS III.

evidence.snippet: Treatment of manifestations: Supportive therapies for neurodevelopmental delays, hearing loss, and visual impairment; medications (rather than behavioral therapy) for psychiatric/behavioral issues; physical therapy and/or orthopedic management of musculoskeletal manifestations; and management as prescribed by consulting specialists for seizures, cardiac involvement, sleep disorders, feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: Sleep disturbance is a common neurobehavioral manifestation across the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type III (MPS III) is a multisystem lysosomal storage disease characterized by progressive central nervous system degeneration manifest as severe intellectual disability (ID), developmental regression, and other neurologic manifestations including autism spectrum disorder (ASD), behavioral problems, and sleep disturbances.

nodes.description: Genistein has preclinical activity as a substrate reduction strategy in MPS IIIB, lowering storage burden, neuroinflammation, and behavioral abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Genistein improves neuropathology and corrects behaviour in a mouse model of neurodegenerative metabolic disease.
evidence.snippet: Genistein may prove useful as a substrate reduction agent to delay clinical onset of MPSIIIB and, due to its multimodal action, may provide a treatment adjunct for several other neurodegenerative metabolic diseases.

The diagnosis of MPS III is established in a proband with suggestive clinical and laboratory findings in whom either biallelic pathogenic variants in one of four genes (GNS, HGSNAT, NAGLU, and SGSH) or deficiency of the respective lysosomal enzyme has been identified.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased SGSH, NAGLU, HGSNAT, or GNS enzyme activity reports the root heparan-sulfate catabolic defect.

nodes.description: Management remains largely supportive, with medications and specialist care directed toward behavioral symptoms, sleep disturbance, seizures, and other complications.

evidence.snippet: Treatment of manifestations: Supportive therapies for neurodevelopmental delays, hearing loss, and visual impairment; medications (rather than behavioral therapy) for psychiatric/behavioral issues; physical therapy and/or orthopedic management of musculoskeletal manifestations; and management as prescribed by consulting specialists for seizures, cardiac involvement, sleep disorders, feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: Although neurologic decline dominates Sanfilippo syndrome, extraneural glycosaminoglycan storage produces a milder systemic branch involving musculoskeletal, auditory, respiratory, and cardiac manifestations.

evidence.snippet: Systemic manifestations can include musculoskeletal problems (joint stiffness, contractures, scoliosis, and hip dysplasia), hearing loss, respiratory tract and sinopulmonary infections, and cardiac disease (valvular thickening, defects in the cardiac conduction system).
Quantitative urinary glycosaminoglycan analysis is strongly recommended, and measurement of disaccharides, heparin cofactor II-thrombin complex and gangliosides is also used.

readouts.interpretation: Abnormal urinary glycosaminoglycan analysis reports the lysosomal glycosaminoglycan storage axis.
